solution:
fn+1-fn=f1

A.P. with common difference = f(1)

General term = Tn = f(1) + (n-1)f(1) = nf(1)

⇒ fn=nf1⇒f is one-one

Now, Let fgx2=fgx1

⇒ gx2=gx1 (as f is one-one)

⇒ x1=x2 (as fog is one-one)

⇒ g is one-one

For f to be onto, f(n) should take all the values of natural numbers.

As f(x) is increasing, f(1)=1

⇒f(n)=n

Now, fgn=gn f1

may be many-one if

gn is many-one

So “if g is onto, then fog is one-one” is incorrect.

and 2x2-3x+43x-5≤1.....4

x∈-12,-13∪12,12.

Total cases will be  5C4×4!

Now favourable cases for 2fb=fc+fd-fa will be,

Case (I) if fb=1 then fc, fd, fa can take the value  3,4,5 & 4,3,5 respectively

Case (II) if fb=2 then fc, fd, fa can take value  3,5,4 & 5,3,4 respectively

Case (III) if fb=3 then fc, fd, fa can take value 2,5,1 & 5,2,1 respectively

So total favourable case will be 6

So probability=favourable casestotal outcomes=65×4!=120

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given $f: \{1, 2, 3, 4\} \rightarrow \{1, e, e^2, e^3\}$ is a strictly decreasing bijective function.

Arranging the domain and codomain in increasing order, we get $f(1) = e^3$, $f(2) = e^2$, $f(3) = e$, and $f(4) = 1$.

Given $g: \{1, e, e^2, e^3\} \rightarrow \left\{1, \dfrac{1}{2}, \dfrac{1}{3}, \dfrac{1}{4}\right\}$ is a strictly increasing bijective function.

Arranging the domain and codomain in increasing order, we get $g(1) = \dfrac{1}{4}$, $g(e) = \dfrac{1}{3}$, $g(e^2) = \dfrac{1}{2}$, and $g(e^3) = 1$.

Evaluating $\phi(x) = \left[f^{-1}\left\{g^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)\right\}\right]^x$:

From the mapping of $g$, $g^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = e^2$.

From the mapping of $f$, $f^{-1}(e^2) = 2$.

Substituting these values, we get $\phi(x) = 2^x$.

The region $R$ is given by $x^2 \leq y \leq 2^x$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$.

The area of the region $R$ is:

$\int_{0}^{1} (2^x - x^2) dx = \left[ \dfrac{2^x}{\log_e(2)} - \dfrac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1}$

$= \left( \dfrac{2}{\log_e(2)} - \dfrac{1}{3} \right) - \left( \dfrac{1}{\log_e(2)} - 0 \right)$

$= \dfrac{1}{\log_e(2)} - \dfrac{1}{3}$

$= \dfrac{3 - \log_e(2)}{3\log_e(2)}$

Answer: $\dfrac{3 - \log_e(2)}{3\log_e(2)}$

options: neither an even nor an odd function
solution:
$f(x)=\log \left(x+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right)$
$f(-x)=-\log \left(x+\sqrt{x^2+1}\right)$
$f(-x)=-f(x)$, i.e., $f(x)$ is an odd function.

subject: Mathematics
Mathematics
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
Statement I: $f(x) = \frac{x}{1+|x|}$. For $x \geq 0$, $f(x) = \frac{x}{1+x}$ is strictly increasing. For $x < 0$, $f(x) = \frac{x}{1-x}$ with $f'(x) = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2} > 0$, also strictly increasing. The function is continuous at $x = 0$, so $f$ is strictly increasing on $\mathbb{R}$ and hence one-one. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $f(x) = \frac{x^2+4x-30}{x^2-8x+18}$. The denominator $(x-4)^2 + 2 > 0$ for all $x$.
$f'(x) = \frac{-12(x^2 - 8x + 14)}{(x^2 - 8x + 18)^2}$
$f'(x) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 4 \pm \sqrt{2}$. Since $f'$ changes sign, $f$ is not monotonic and hence many-one. Statement II is true.
Both statements are true.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given the set A={0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}.

And, also a function f:A→A satisfying f(1)+f(2)=3-f(3)

⇒ f(1)+f(2)+f(3)=3

Since, the range of the function is also A, hence the only possibility satisfying the given condition is: 0+1+2=3

We know that, the number of arrangements of n objects at n places is n!.

Since, the given function is bijective i.e. one-one and onto, hence, the elements 1, 2, 3 in the domain can be mapped with only 0, 1, 2 in the co-domain in 3! ways and the remaining 5 elements 0, 4, 5, 6, 7 in the domain can be mapped with any of the remaining 5 elements 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 in 5! ways. 

So, the number of bijective functions are =3!×5!=6×120=720.

⇒x∈[-6,3)-2

So, on comparing with x∈[-α,β)-γ we get,

⇒α+β+γ=6+3+2=11

subject: Mathematics
solution:
For set $A$:
$|(|x-3|-3)| \le 1 \Rightarrow 2 \le |x-3| \le 4$.
If $x \ge 3$: $5 \le x \le 7 \Rightarrow x \in \{5,6,7\}$.
If $x < 3$: $-1 \le x \le 1 \Rightarrow x \in \{-1,0,1\}$.
So $A = \{-1,0,1,5,6,7\}$, $|A|=6$.
For set $B$:
$\frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{x-1}\log_e(|x-2|) = 0$ with $x \in \mathbb{R}-\{1,2\}$.
Either $\frac{(x-2)(x-4)}{x-1}=0 \Rightarrow x=4$ (since $x \neq 1,2$), or $\log_e|x-2|=0 \Rightarrow |x-2|=1 \Rightarrow x=3$ (since $x \neq 1$).
So $B = \{3,4\}$, $|B|=2$.
Number of onto functions from $A$ to $B$ $= 2^6 - 2 = 62$.

$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } g(x)=x^2-4 x+9 \\ & D < 0 \\ & g(x)>0 \text { for } x \in R\end{aligned}$
$\therefore\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{f}(-5)=0 \\ \mathrm{f}(3)=0 \end{array}\right.$
So, $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})$ is many-one. again, $\begin{aligned} & y x^2-4 x y+9 y=x^2+2 x-15 \\ & x^2(y-1)-2 x(2 y+1)+(9 y+15)=0 \\ & \text { for } \forall x \in R \Rightarrow D \geq 0 \\ & D=4(2 y+1)^2-4(y-1)(9 y+15) \geq 0 \\ & 5 y^2+2 y+16 \leq 0 \\ & (5 y-8)(y+2) \leq 0 \end{aligned}$

solution:
Given that fx=log4x2+11x+6+sin-14x+3+cos-110x+63

We know that the domain of Ax+Bx+Cx is DA∩DB∩DC.

Let us find the domain of log4x2+11x+6.

We know that domain of logfx is fx>0.

⇒4x2+11x+6>0

⇒4x2+8x+3x+6>0

⇒4x+3x+2>0

⇒x∈-∞,-2∪-34,∞

Domain of log4x2+11x+9 is x∈-∞,-2∪-34,∞.

Let us find the domain of sin-14x+3

⇒-1≤4x+3≤1

⇒-1≤x≤-12

⇒x∈-1,-12

Let us find the domain of cos-110x+63.

⇒-1≤10x+63≤1

⇒-3≤10x+6≤3

⇒-910≤x≤-310.

Now the common domain is -34≤x≤-12.

⇒x∈-34,-12

But given that the domain of fx is x∈α,β.

⇒α=-34, β=-12.

⇒36α+β=36-34+-12

⇒36α+β=36×-54

⇒36α+β=45

Therefore, the required answer is 45.

solution:
Given, f( x )=x−5[ x 5 ]

Hence, f( x )=5( x 5 −[ x 5 ] )

=5{ x 5 }

Since, x is a natural number. 

Hence, x5=0,15,25,35,45

Hence, f( x )=0 , when x=5n, n∈N

=1 when x=5n+1, n∈N

=2 when x=5n+2, n∈N

=3 when x=5n+3, n∈N

=4 when x=5n+4, n∈N

Hence, fx is neither one-one nor onto.
